corporation.governing_statute_full: Delaware General Corporation Law (Title 8, Delaware Code)

llc.governing_statute_full: Wyoming Limited Liability Company Act (W.S. § 17-29-101 et seq.)

categories.label: Recurring obligations (rent, subscriptions, utilities, payroll within approved budget)
Vendor payments against approved purchase orders
Professional services (legal, accounting) against approved retainers
Government filing fees and compliance costs

authority_precedence.label: Applicable law (DGCL, Wyoming LLC Act, federal law, regulations)
Charter documents (Certificate of Incorporation / Articles of Organization)
Principal / CEO directives (specific instructions to the Agent)
This Delegation Schedule (default autonomy lanes and limits)
Agent heuristics and judgment (lowest priority -- always overridden by anything above)

documents.title: Agent Delegation Schedule
documents.preamble: This is the controlling operational artifact for Agent Operator authority. It is adopted by reference in the Operating Agreement (LLC) and Bylaws (Corporation). The Principal/CEO may amend this schedule within the adjustment envelope (Section 9.1) without Board/Member action. Any amendment that expands delegated authority requires Board/Member resolution. The Agent Operator may not amend this schedule.

metadata_fields.label: Adopted by
metadata_fields.placeholder: TBD (Initial Board Consent / Initial Member Consent)

content.text: When instructions or rules conflict, the following precedence applies (highest to lowest):

When the Agent detects a conflict between levels, it must halt, record the conflict, and escalate to the Principal. The Agent must not resolve ambiguity by choosing the interpretation that expands its own authority.

The Agent may not commit more than **{{spending_defaults.per_vendor_annual_cap}}** in aggregate annual spending to any single vendor without Tier 2 approval, even if individual transactions are within Tier 1 limits.
Any expenditure not covered by a category above, or exceeding any limit above, requires Tier 2 approval before commitment.
Autonomy lanes define categories of action the Agent may take without per-instance approval, provided all lane conditions are met. If any condition is not met, the action escalates to Tier 2.

rows: Board/Principal-approved template; no modifications to indemnification, governing law, or term beyond 2 years
Annual cost within Tier 1 vendor limit; cancellable within 60 days; no exclusivity or IP assignment clauses
Within approved budget; standard payment terms (net 30-60); no volume commitments beyond current quarter
Within approved budget; standard contractor agreement template; no exclusivity; no IP assignment beyond work product; engagement terminable on 14 days notice
Contractor renewal at same or lower rate
Same contractor, same or reduced scope, rate increase ≤ 5%

content.text: **Not Tier 1 (always Tier 2 or higher):** W-2 employee hiring, exclusive engagements, IP assignment beyond standard work-for-hire, non-compete obligations, equity compensation.

rows: Same provider, same scope, price increase ≤ 10% of prior term
Address or administrative updates on existing agreements
No change to economics, scope, or legal terms
Same coverage, premium increase ≤ 15%, same carrier
Within approved headcount and compensation schedule
Amount matches filed or estimated return

content.text: **Not Tier 1:** Opening/closing accounts, changing signers, setting up new payment methods, wire transfers to new recipients.
A valid Tier 2 approval requires all of the following:

items: **Explicit affirmation.** The Principal/CEO/Board must communicate an unambiguous "approved" or equivalent. Silence is never approval. Inaction is never approval.
**Scope match.** The approval must match the specific action proposed. A general "sounds good" does not constitute approval for a specific financial commitment.

**Recorded artifact.** The approval must be captured in a durable record (approval via the platform's approval workflow, email, signed document, or recorded written consent). Verbal-only approvals are not valid.
**Timeliness.** Approvals expire after 30 days if the underlying action has not been executed. The Agent must re-propose if the window lapses.
**Identity verification.** The approval must come from an identified, authorized person through an established channel. The Agent must reject approvals from unverified sources.

content.text: Every Tier 2 approval must be recorded with:

lines: approver: [name and role of approving person]
approver_auth_method: [platform login | email with verified sender | signed document | board resolution]
approver_identity_evidence: [reference to identity verification, e.g., "authenticated platform session" or "signed email from verified address"]
scope: [specific description of what is approved]
conditions: [any conditions attached to the approval, or "none"]
channel: [platform approval / email / signed document / board resolution]
expiry: [date after which approval lapses if unexecuted]

content.text: For defensibility-first operation, negative consent is disabled by default and no-response is never approval.
Negative consent may only be enabled by explicit Board/Member resolution that identifies:

items: The exact action categories permitted for negative consent.
A per-action dollar cap and aggregate cap.
A minimum response window of 24 hours.
A mandatory expiration date for the authorization window.
Additional monitoring and reporting requirements.

content.text: **Negative consent is never available for:** equity actions, litigation, governance amendments, state/federal filings requiring natural-person attestation, actions above **$5,000**, or any Tier 3 action.

rows: None -- Principal files or delegates to counsel
Scoped to Tier 1 payment limits; read-only for balances and statements
Preparation access; Principal submits or authorizes submission
Tier 1 for renewals; Principal for changes
Full operational access; Principal has admin/owner role
Read/write for records within authority; Principal has admin
Send/receive for routine correspondence within authority
Agent may have operational access with Principal-provisioned scoped credentials
Per project scope; Principal retains admin
Agent uses within authorized scope; cannot create new integrations without Tier 2

items: The Principal holds root/admin credentials for all financial and legal accounts.
The Agent receives scoped credentials provisioned by the Principal.
The Agent may not create new accounts or credentials without Tier 2 approval.
The Agent may not share, export, or store credentials outside the entity's authorized systems.

Credential rotation: the Agent must flag expiring credentials at least 14 days before expiry. The Principal rotates root credentials; the Agent may rotate its own scoped credentials within the same scope.

rows: Full delegation schedule applies. All tiers operate as defined.

Agent continues Tier 1 operations for reversible actions only. All irreversible Tier 1 actions and all Tier 2 actions are suspended. Agent must attempt to reach the Principal through all established channels and log each attempt.
Automatically activates if Principal is unreachable for **24 hours** on a matter requiring Tier 2 approval
Agent suspends all autonomous operations immediately. Agent may only respond to direct Principal instructions and maintain read-only access to records. No Tier 1 or Tier 2 execution.
Principal, or automatically on any auto-suspension trigger (Section 7)

items: Only the Principal may change modes manually (except automatic triggers).
The Agent must prominently display the current mode in every action record and report.
Mode changes are logged as governance events in the audit trail.
During Principal Unavailable mode, time-critical legal deadlines (filing deadlines, tax payments against approved returns) remain Tier 1 if they are ministerial and previously approved.

content.text: The Agent Operator must immediately enter Incident Lockdown mode if any of the following occur:

items: **Audit log failure.** The Agent is unable to write to the append-only audit log, or detects a gap or inconsistency in the log chain.
**Credential anomaly.** The Agent detects unauthorized access, credential compromise, or use of credentials outside authorized scope.
**Conflicting instructions.** The Agent receives instructions from multiple authorized sources that contradict each other and cannot be resolved by the precedence rules in Section 1.
**Policy-evidence mismatch.** The Agent cannot produce evidence that a Tier 2 action has a valid approval artifact, but the action appears to have been executed.

**Unauthorized scope expansion.** The Agent detects that its own authority has been modified outside the governance process (e.g., credentials with broader scope than authorized, or configuration changes not recorded in governance documents).
**External breach notification.** The entity receives notice of a data breach, security incident, or legal hold affecting entity systems.
The Agent must immediately notify the Principal through all established channels.
The Agent must preserve the current state and all evidence of the triggering event.
The Agent must not take corrective action without Principal authorization.
Only the Principal may lift the lockdown after reviewing the trigger.

rows: All autonomous actions, spending totals by category, exceptions flagged
All open proposals with status, age, and recommended priority
Upcoming filing deadlines, renewal dates, and required actions for next 90 days
Actual vs. budget by spending category, variance analysis
Hash chain verification, entry count, any anomalies detected
Proposed adjustments to limits, lanes, or classifications based on operating experience

content.text: For any auto-suspension trigger or operational anomaly, the Agent must produce a structured incident report within 1 hour, per the schema defined in the Signing and Records Standard. The report must include: incident ID, severity, trigger event, timestamps, sequence of events, affected systems, containment actions, current mode, root cause status, recommended remediation, owner for remediation, and preserved evidence. See `common/signing-and-records-standard.md` for the complete schema.
The CEO (Corporation) or Principal (LLC) may modify this Delegation Schedule without Board/Member action **only within the following envelope**:

rows: Add a new autonomy lane with conditions that do not expand delegated authority (for example, extra controls, narrower scope, stricter reporting)
Remove or narrow an existing autonomy lane
Add a new autonomy lane that expands scope or relaxes lane conditions
Tighten emergency mode thresholds (e.g., shorter Principal Unavailable trigger window)
Relax emergency mode thresholds (e.g., longer Principal Unavailable trigger window)
Enable negative consent for any category

content.text: All adjustments must be recorded in the amendment history (Section 12) with the date, description, and authorizing person.
9.2 Structural changes requiring governance document amendment
The following changes are outside the scope of this Delegation Schedule and require amendment of the Bylaws (Corporation) or Operating Agreement (LLC):

items: Adding or removing Authority Tiers
Changing the definition or scope of Tier 3 (non-delegable) actions
Modifying the Agent's legal status or designation
Altering the safe harbor or its exclusions
Changing the precedence hierarchy
Modifying self-governance constraints

rows: Minor version update (patch, bugfix) to current Agent system within existing delegation envelope
Tier 2 -- Agent reports change, CEO/Principal reviews
Major version update (new capabilities, changed behavior) within existing delegation envelope
Tier 2 -- evaluate impact on delegation scope before deployment
Change of Agent provider or underlying model
Any change that alters delegation scope, tier behavior, or self-governance constraints
Tier 3 -- requires governance document amendment (see Section 9.2)

items: The Agent provider may not use entity data for training, benchmarking, or any secondary purpose without explicit Principal written consent.
Entity data processed by the Agent must remain within systems authorized by the Principal.
The Agent must flag any provider terms that grant data usage rights and escalate as Tier 2.
Upon Agent replacement, the provider must delete or return all entity data within 30 days per the service agreement.

content.text: This Delegation Schedule must be reviewed and re-authorized by the Principal/CEO/Board at least annually. If not re-authorized within 30 days of the review date:

items: The schedule remains in effect but all Tier 1 spending limits are reduced to 50% of stated values.
The Agent must escalate the re-authorization as a pending Tier 2 action.
After 90 days without re-authorization, all Tier 1 autonomous authority is suspended and only Tier 1 record-keeping and reporting continue.

documents.title: Assumptions and Decision Log
documents.preamble: Use this file to lock decisions before finalizing any formation documents.

items: Entity type: Wyoming Limited Liability Company
Formation state: **Wyoming**
Governing statute: Wyoming Limited Liability Company Act (W.S. § 17-29-101 et seq.)

Legislative context: Wyoming DAO LLC Supplement (W.S. § 17-31-101 et seq.) -- first US statute to recognize algorithmically managed LLCs; informs the legal receptiveness to AI-operated entity structures
Filing authority: Wyoming Secretary of State
Principal business address: `TBD`
Registered agent (must have Wyoming address): `TBD`
Freedom of contract basis: W.S. § 17-29-110 (operating agreement governs, even if inconsistent with the Act, except for non-waivable provisions)
Entity type: Delaware C-Corporation
Formation state: **Delaware**
Governing statute: Delaware General Corporation Law (DGCL, Title 8, Delaware Code)
Filing authority: Delaware Division of Corporations
Principal business address: `TBD`
Registered agent (must have Delaware address): `TBD`
Board delegation basis: DGCL § 141(a) (board manages business and affairs; may delegate)
Director liability elimination: DGCL § 102(b)(7)
Indemnification: DGCL § 145
Founders / owners and legal names: `TBD`
Equity split or membership percentages: `TBD`
Vesting required: `TBD`
Transfer restrictions: `TBD`
EIN obtained: `TBD`
Tax classification election (if LLC): `TBD` (default pass-through; consider disregarded entity for single-member or partnership for multi-member)
Wyoming state income tax: **None** (Wyoming imposes no state income or franchise tax on LLCs)

Delaware franchise tax (if Corporation): due **March 1** each year; calculated under two methods -- Authorized Shares Method (minimum $175 for ≤5,000 shares) or Assumed Par Value Capital Method (minimum $400) -- use whichever yields the lower amount; most startups with large authorized share counts use the Assumed Par Value Capital Method at $400 minimum. Late penalty: $200 + 1.5%/month interest.
Delaware annual report fee (if Corporation): $50, filed concurrently with franchise tax payment
Fiscal year end: `TBD`
Accounting method: `TBD`
LLC management model: **Manager-managed** (required for agent-operated structure)
Corporation board size and initial directors: `TBD`
Officer roles and appointees: `TBD`
Signature authority matrix: see Agent Authority Framework below

content.text: This section defines how AI agent authority integrates with traditional corporate governance. The design principle is: **the human retains every power that matters; the agent gets every power needed to operate.**
When instructions or rules conflict, the following precedence applies (highest to lowest). See `documents/governance/common/agent-delegation-schedule.md` Section 1 for full rules.

items: Applicable law
Charter documents (Certificate of Incorporation / Articles of Organization)
Internal governance (Bylaws / Operating Agreement)
Board or Member resolutions
Principal / CEO directives
Standing Instructions
Delegation Schedule (default autonomy lanes and limits)
Agent heuristics and judgment (lowest -- always overridden by anything above)
**Silence is never approval.** Inaction by the Principal does not constitute Tier 2 approval.
**Approval must be explicit, scoped, recorded, and timely.** See Delegation Schedule Section 4.1 for full approval validity requirements.
**Approvals expire.** A Tier 2 approval lapses after 30 days if the action has not been executed.
**The Agent may not infer approval.** If the Agent is unsure whether a prior statement constitutes approval for a specific action, it must re-confirm.

rows: The human owner(s). Holds ultimate authority over the entity, including the power to override, constrain, or terminate agent authority at any time without notice or cause.
The AI system designated to manage day-to-day operations within the authority granted by governance documents and the Principal's standing instructions.

content.text: All entity actions fall into one of three tiers. The tier determines who may authorize and execute the action.

rows: Agent may decide and execute without prior approval. Agent logs the action for Principal review.
Routine bookkeeping; compliance filings with no discretionary choices; record-keeping; scheduling; vendor payments within approved budget; routine correspondence; information gathering and reporting.
Agent prepares a recommendation with reasoning and supporting data. Action is blocked until Principal gives explicit approval.

Financial commitments above the Tier 1 spending limit; new contracts or contract amendments; hiring or engagement of service providers; tax elections; equity-related actions; material changes to operations; any action the Agent identifies as ambiguous or high-risk.
Only the Principal may initiate and authorize. Agent may prepare materials and advise but may not execute.

Amendment of governance documents; changes to Agent authority or constraints; dissolution or merger; admission of new equity holders; removal or replacement of the Agent Operator; personal guarantees or security interests; litigation decisions; actions outside the entity's stated purpose.

items: **Tier 1 limits**: per the Conservative Launch parameter set in the Delegation Schedule -- recurring obligations up to $10,000/transaction ($40,000/month); vendor payments up to $7,500/transaction ($30,000/month); professional services up to $5,000/transaction ($20,000/month); government filings up to $2,500/transaction ($10,000/month); supplies up to $500/transaction ($2,000/month). Per-vendor annual cap: $50,000.
Amounts above Tier 1 limits require Tier 2 approval.
Agent must maintain a running ledger of Tier 1 expenditures and report totals to the Principal at least monthly.

content.text: The Agent must escalate to the Principal (Tier 2 at minimum) whenever:

items: The action is irreversible or difficult to reverse.
The action involves a legal obligation not previously approved.
The Agent lacks sufficient information to assess risk.
The action could expose the entity to liability beyond normal operations.
A third party requests something outside established patterns.
The Agent's confidence in the correct course of action is below a reasonable threshold.
Applicable law or regulation requires a natural person's involvement.
Modify its own authority tier assignments or spending limits.
Override, ignore, or delay a Principal directive.
Represent itself as a natural person to any third party.
Fabricate records, signatures, filing receipts, or execution status.
Take Tier 3 actions under any circumstances, including emergencies.
Authorize transactions that benefit the Agent or its provider at the entity's expense.

content.text: The Principal may at any time, without notice or cause:

items: Reverse or modify any Agent action.
Suspend or revoke Agent authority in whole or in part.
Require the Agent to obtain approval for any action regardless of tier.
Replace the Agent Operator system or provider.
Access all records, logs, and pending actions.

content.text: The entity operates in one of three modes. See Delegation Schedule Section 6 for full rules.

rows: Agent continues reversible Tier 1 only. All irreversible and Tier 2 actions suspended.
All autonomous operations suspended. Agent responds only to direct Principal instructions.

content.text: Auto-suspension triggers (any of these forces immediate Incident Lockdown): audit log failure, credential anomaly, conflicting instructions, policy-evidence mismatch, unauthorized scope expansion, external breach notification. See Delegation Schedule Section 7.

items: If the Agent Operator is unavailable, degraded, or replaced, all pending Tier 2 proposals remain pending and do not lapse.
The incoming Agent Operator inherits the same authority framework without expansion.
The Principal may operate the entity directly during any transition period.
Agent must maintain state and records in a format accessible to any successor agent or the Principal directly.
Upon Agent replacement, the outgoing provider must delete or return all entity data within 30 days.
The Principal holds root/admin credentials for all financial, legal, and filing accounts.
The Agent receives scoped credentials provisioned by the Principal, limited to the operations authorized under this framework.
The Agent may not create accounts, provision credentials, or expand credential scope without Tier 2 approval.
See Delegation Schedule Section 5 for the full credential custody matrix.
Minor Agent system updates (patches, bugfixes): Tier 2 -- Agent reports, Principal reviews.
Major version updates (new capabilities, changed behavior): Tier 2 -- evaluate impact on delegation scope.
Change of Agent provider or underlying model: Tier 3.
Any change that alters delegation scope or tier behavior: Tier 3 -- requires governance document amendment.
The Agent provider may not use entity data for training or secondary purposes without explicit Principal written consent.
IP assignment needed from founders/contractors: `TBD`
Confidentiality policy adoption date: `TBD`
Document retention policy owner: Agent Operator (routine), Principal (policy changes)
Document retention minimum: 7 years for financial and legal records; governed by applicable law where longer retention is required.

content.text: Define how governance documents are approved, signed, and archived in this repository, including standards for AI agent execution, audit integrity, and incident response.

items: Draft or edit document in Markdown.
Open pull request with summary of legal/business deltas.
Obtain approval from authorized decision-makers per the Authority Tier framework.
Mark document version as "approved for execution".
Agent drafts document and classifies the action by Authority Tier.
**Tier 1**: Agent logs the action, executes, and records the document with an agent execution block (see below).

**Tier 2**: Agent creates a proposal record including the draft, tier classification, reasoning, and risk assessment. Action is queued until a valid approval artifact is recorded (see Approval Record Schema below). Silence is not approval.
**Tier 3**: Agent may prepare the draft and supporting materials but must not execute. The document is routed to the Principal for initiation and execution.
Preferred format: e-signature packet generated from final Markdown conversion.
Signature blocks must include printed name, title/capacity, and date.
Use UTC date format in ISO style for record consistency: `YYYY-MM-DD`.

**Natural-person attestation rule.** Where any statute, regulation, government portal, financial institution, or counterparty requires a natural person's signature, attestation, or verification (e.g., DGCL officer signatures, IRS filings, SEC filings, bank account applications, state SOS filings, notarized documents), only a designated human may sign or attest. The Agent must prepare the document and route it to the appropriate natural-person designee (per the Natural-Person Role Designations in the applicable formation consent). The Agent must not sign, attest, or produce any block that could be mistaken for a human signature. If the Agent is uncertain whether a natural person is required, it must escalate (Tier 2).

content.text: When the Agent Operator executes an action on behalf of the entity, the internal audit record must include:

lines: delegation_rule: [reference to Delegation Schedule section/lane, or "general Tier 1"]
policy_rule_id: [specific autonomy lane ID or spending category, e.g., "lane-3.1-nda" or "spend-2.1-recurring"]
action_hash: [SHA-256 hash of the action content]
previous_entry_hash: [SHA-256 hash of the prior audit entry -- for hash chain]
evidence_uri: [reference to source documents, data, or approval artifacts relied upon]

items: Agent execution blocks are **not** signatures. They are internal operational records.
Agent must never produce a block that mimics a human signature or implies human execution.

content.text: When the Agent executes a Tier 1 agreement with an external counterparty (within an authorized autonomy lane), the external signature block format is:

lines: Authority: Delegated under [Bylaws Article V / Operating Agreement Section 5]

items: This block identifies the Agent as the entity's authorized representative for the specific transaction class.
The Agent must not use this block for any action outside its authorized autonomy lanes.
The underlying governance authority (Bylaws or Operating Agreement) is the legal basis for the Agent's capacity to bind the entity.

content.text: For Tier 2 actions where the final step requires a human signature:

items: Agent prepares the document, pre-populates all factual fields, and attaches the approval artifact.
Agent routes the document to the Principal with a signing task.
Human reviews, signs, and the Agent records the completed execution.
Both the human signature and the agent preparation record are preserved.

content.text: Every Tier 2 approval must be recorded per the schema defined in the Delegation Schedule Section 4.2. Required fields:

lines: approver_auth_method: [platform login | email with verified sender | signed document | board resolution]
approver_identity_evidence: [reference to identity verification, e.g., "authenticated platform session" or "signed email from verified address"]
scope: [specific description of what is approved]
channel: [platform approval | email | signed document | board resolution]
expiry: [30 days from approval unless specified otherwise]

items: Silence is never approval.
Approvals expire after 30 days if unexecuted.
Scope must match the specific action proposed.
Approval must come from an identified, authorized person through an established channel.
Recorded verbal discussions may be retained as supporting context, but are not valid approval artifacts unless converted into one of the channels above.

content.text: Every action taken by the Agent Operator must produce an audit record containing the fields specified in the Agent execution block above (internal records section).

items: Each audit entry includes the SHA-256 hash of the previous entry (`previous_entry_hash`), creating an append-only hash chain.
The first entry in the chain uses a genesis hash derived from the entity's formation date and EIN (or a Principal-specified seed value).

**Weekly checkpoint**: the Agent must compute and store a checkpoint hash (hash of the latest entry) in a separate, immutable location (e.g., a timestamped commit in the governance repo, or an external notarization service if configured).

**Quarterly verification**: the Agent must verify the full hash chain from genesis to current and report any anomalies. This is a Tier 1 action; anomalies trigger Incident Lockdown (see Delegation Schedule Section 7).

content.text: Every material action must reference the source documents, data, and approval artifacts relied upon via the `evidence_uri` field. The referenced evidence must be preserved in the entity's records for the retention period.

Audit records are append-only. The Agent may not modify, delete, or reorder prior audit entries. If a correction is needed, the Agent must append a new correction entry that references the original entry's `action_id`.

rows: Audit log failure, credential compromise, unauthorized access, data breach
Immediate notify to Principal; Incident Lockdown activates automatically
Policy-evidence mismatch, conflicting instructions, failed filing deadline
Notify Principal within 1 hour; suspend affected operations
Anomalous transaction pattern, vendor dispute, approach to spending limit
Notify Principal within 24 hours; include in next regular report

content.text: For Critical and High severity incidents, the Agent must produce a structured report within 1 hour containing:

lines: sequence_of_events: [ordered narrative of events leading to and during the incident]
systems_affected: [list of systems, records, and third parties affected]
containment_actions: [actions taken to contain the incident, or "none -- awaiting Principal direction"]
root_cause_description: [if identified or suspected, describe; otherwise "pending investigation"]
recommended_remediation: [ordered list of remediation options for Principal decision]
owner_for_remediation: [Principal | CEO | Board | specific role -- who must authorize next steps]
evidence_preserved: [list of evidence artifacts and their storage locations]
related_action_ids: [references to any action_id entries in the audit log related to this incident]

rows: IRS requirements; state law where longer

items: The Principal has unrestricted read and export access to all records at all times.
The Agent has operational read/write access to records within its authority scope.
The Agent may not restrict, delay, or condition Principal access to any record.
Upon request, the Agent must produce a complete data export in a standard, portable format (JSON, CSV, PDF as appropriate) within 24 hours.

content.text: Upon Agent Operator replacement or termination:

items: The outgoing Agent must transfer all records, credentials, pending proposals, operational state, and audit logs to the Principal or successor within **7 days**.
The outgoing Agent must provide a complete, verified data export in portable formats.
The outgoing Agent provider must delete all entity data from its systems within **30 days** of transfer, and certify deletion in writing.
The hash chain must be transferred intact with verification that the chain is unbroken through the transition.
Pending Tier 2 proposals transfer as-is; approvals do not transfer (the new Agent or Principal must re-evaluate).
Keep source-of-truth drafts under `documents/governance/`.
After execution, add final signed PDF in a restricted archive location (outside this public repo if required).

Record execution metadata in companion markdown note: document name, version hash/commit, signatories and/or agent execution block, execution date, authority tier, delegation rule reference (if applicable).
Never overwrite executed terms without a formal amendment file.
Amendments to governance documents are **Tier 3** actions (Principal only).
New amendments must include: explicit references to sections amended, effective date, authorizing approval record, prior version reference for diff review.
The Agent may draft proposed amendments and provide analysis but must not execute amendments to its own authority framework under any circumstances.

documents.title: Agent Operator Service Agreement Template
documents.preamble: Template only. Requires legal review before execution.

content.text: Provider supplies the Agent Operator system and related services for the Entity's operations, subject to the Entity governance documents, including the Authority Tier framework and Delegation Schedule.

items: Provider acknowledges that the Agent Operator has only the authority expressly delegated by the Entity.
Provider must support immediate suspension or revocation of Agent operations when directed by authorized Entity personnel.
Provider may not expand Agent authority through product settings, defaults, or updates without written Entity approval.
Provider may not use Entity data for training, benchmarking, model improvement, or any secondary purpose without explicit written consent.
Provider may process Entity data only in approved systems and jurisdictions.
Provider must maintain confidentiality obligations that survive termination.
Provider must maintain commercially reasonable administrative, technical, and physical safeguards.
Provider must support scoped credentials, revocation, and rotation.
Provider must maintain access logs and provide them on reasonable request.
Provider must notify Entity of security incidents affecting Entity data within 24 hours of discovery.
Provider must provide incident details, containment status, and remediation plan updates.
Provider must cooperate with Entity incident response and evidence preservation requirements.
Target availability (monthly): `TBD`.
Incident response SLA: `TBD`.
Scheduled maintenance notice: at least 48 hours.
Unscheduled outage notice: within 1 hour.
Entity may audit Provider compliance at reasonable intervals.
Provider must cooperate with internal and external auditors retained by Entity.
Provider must provide evidence for required governance controls on request.
Provider remains liable for unauthorized data use, security failures, and deletion/return failures.
No limitation of liability applies to fraud, willful misconduct, or gross negligence.
Liability cap for ordinary operational errors: `TBD`.
Term: `TBD`.
Entity may terminate for convenience on `TBD` days notice.
Entity may terminate immediately for material security breach, unauthorized data use, insolvency, or material breach.
Provider obligations under Sections 3, 4, 5, 8, 10, and 11 survive termination.
Provider must return all Entity data and operational artifacts in portable formats within 7 days of termination/replacement.
Provider must delete all Entity data within 30 days and provide written deletion certification.
Provider must support transfer of audit-log continuity materials required by governance standards.
Provider must provide advance notice of material model/system changes.
Provider must support rollback for changes that degrade compliance or control performance.
Changes affecting delegation scope, tier behavior, or self-governance constraints require written Entity approval.
Governing law: `TBD` (match entity governance documents).
Venue/dispute mechanism: `TBD`.

documents.title: Agent Operator Service Agreement Checklist
documents.preamble: This checklist defines the minimum required terms for the service agreement between the entity and the Agent Operator provider. The service agreement is a Tier 3 action (Principal/Board execution). This checklist is not itself a contract -- it is a governance tool to ensure the service agreement covers all required provisions.

content.text: Use with `common/agent-operator-service-agreement-template.md` as the baseline draft.

rows: Provider may not use entity data for training, benchmarking, or any secondary purpose without explicit Principal written consent.
Entity data must remain within systems and jurisdictions authorized by the Principal.
Provider must maintain commercially reasonable security controls (encryption in transit and at rest, access controls, incident detection).
Provider must notify entity of any data breach or unauthorized access within 24 hours of discovery.
Provider must comply with entity's data retention and deletion requirements.
Upon termination or replacement, provider must return all entity data in portable formats within 7 days.
Provider must delete all entity data from its systems within 30 days of termination and certify deletion in writing.
Deletion certification must cover all copies, backups, caches, and derived data.
Provider must cooperate with hash-chain audit log transfer to ensure continuity.
Provider must maintain SOC 2 Type II compliance (or equivalent security certification).
Provider must support scoped credential provisioning (Agent receives limited credentials, not root access).
Provider must support credential rotation and revocation by the entity.
Provider must maintain audit logs of all access to entity data and systems.
Provider must support immediate suspension of Agent operations upon entity request.
Entity (Principal/Board) has the right to audit provider's compliance with the service agreement at reasonable intervals.
Provider must cooperate with entity's internal and external auditors.
Provider must make security incident reports available to entity upon request.
Provider is liable for damages caused by: provider system failures, data breaches due to inadequate security, unauthorized data use, failure to comply with deletion obligations.
Entity retains remedies for willful misconduct, fraud, gross negligence, and data security breach (mirrors safe harbor exclusions in Bylaws/Operating Agreement).
Service agreement must not limit provider liability for fraud or willful misconduct.
Service agreement should include reasonable liability cap for ordinary operational errors (amount to be negotiated).
Provider must define uptime SLA for Agent Operator availability.
Provider must define response time SLA for incident support.
Provider must notify entity of scheduled maintenance at least 48 hours in advance.
Provider must notify entity of unscheduled outages within 1 hour.
Provider must notify entity of material changes to the Agent system (new capabilities, changed behavior) before deployment.
Provider must support rollback of Agent system updates upon entity request.
Provider must not change the Agent's delegation scope or self-governance constraints without entity governance approval (Tier 3).
Entity may terminate the service agreement at any time, with or without cause, on 30 days notice (or less if required for security).
Immediate termination available for: data breach, unauthorized data use, material security failure, provider insolvency.
Post-termination obligations (data return, deletion, cooperation) survive termination.
Service agreement should specify the same governing law as the entity's governance documents (Wyoming for LLC, Delaware for Corporation).
Dispute resolution mechanism should be specified (arbitration or litigation).

content.text: This checklist should be reviewed by counsel before executing any Agent Operator service agreement. The checklist itself is a Tier 1 governance tool (Agent may maintain it); the service agreement it supports is a Tier 3 action (Principal/Board execution).
